settings.title: Editorial System for Meridian Press
settings.summary: We designed a grid and type system that lets a small publisher lay out a quarterly journal in half the time.
settings.description:
Meridian put out a quarterly arts journal, but every issue was laid out from scratch and looked a little different each time. With a small team and a hard print deadline four times a year, that meant long nights nudging columns into place and a title that never quite settled into a recognizable look.

The brief

Meridian did not want a rigid template that flattened every article into the same shape. An arts journal needs room to breathe and the odd surprise. The ask was a system with structure underneath and freedom on top, that a small team could work fast.

What we built

We built a reusable editorial system around a flexible grid, a pairing of two typefaces, and a set of templates for the recurring sections. The grid gives every page a shared backbone while still allowing a feature to open large and a review to run tight.

  • A flexible baseline grid that holds the whole journal together
  • A two-typeface pairing for headings and body
  • Templates for features, reviews, and front matter
  • Spacing and style rules the team can apply without us

The two-typeface pairing did a lot of quiet work. By limiting choices to one display face and one text face, we removed most of the per-issue decisions that used to eat time and drift the look. Designers now reach for a defined option instead of inventing one.

The result

Layout time for each issue dropped by about half, and the journal finally looks like one consistent title across the run. The team spends less of its deadline week fighting the layout and more of it on the writing and images, which is where it wanted to be.
